# Remote Access

How to reach the cluster and nodes from a workstation. **This is the primary access path** — the workstation is not assumed to be on the node LAN.

## TL;DR

```sh
# Talos (node management) — via Tailscale MagicDNS hostnames
talosctl --talosconfig ./talos/clusterconfig/talosconfig \
  -e k8s-1 -n k8s-1,k8s-2,k8s-3 <command>

# Kubernetes — via kubeconfig generated by task bootstrap / talhelper
kubectl get nodes
```

## Access Paths

The cluster nodes (`k8s-1`, `k8s-2`, `k8s-3`) are reachable two ways:

1. **LAN** — the `192.168.x.x` addresses in `talos/talconfig.yaml`. Only works when the workstation is on the home network.
2. **Tailscale** — every node runs a Tailscale extension and is advertised on the tailnet as a MagicDNS host: `k8s-1`, `k8s-2`, `k8s-3`. **This is the default remote path.** Prefer MagicDNS hostnames over Tailscale `100.x.x.x` IPs in any output that might be saved or shared — the IPs are identifying.

The Tailscale extension is configured per-node in `talos/patches/<hostname>/` (see `talos/talconfig.yaml` for the extension image and auth-key secret wiring).

## talosctl

The generated talosconfig at `talos/clusterconfig/talosconfig` is **gitignored** (produced by `task talos:generate-config`). It bakes in the LAN IPs as default endpoints, so when working off-LAN you must override with `-e`:

```sh
# Single node
talosctl --talosconfig ./talos/clusterconfig/talosconfig -e k8s-1 -n k8s-1 <cmd>

# Fan out to all nodes (pick any reachable node as the -e endpoint)
talosctl --talosconfig ./talos/clusterconfig/talosconfig \
  -e k8s-1 -n k8s-1,k8s-2,k8s-3 <cmd>
```

Common read-only queries:

```sh
talosctl ... get disks        # block devices (NVMe, loops, rbd)
talosctl ... get members      # cluster membership
talosctl ... get addresses    # interface addresses
talosctl ... version          # server vs client version skew
talosctl ... dmesg -f         # kernel log (follow)
talosctl ... logs <service>   # machined/kubelet/etcd/etc.
```

If talosctl errors with `failed to determine endpoints` or `talos config file is empty`, the default `~/.talos/config` has no context set — always pass `--talosconfig ./talos/clusterconfig/talosconfig` explicitly from the repo root.

## kubectl

Kubernetes API access also flows over Tailscale when remote. The `cluster_api_addr` in `cluster.yaml` is a LAN VIP, so the kubeconfig server URL may need to point at a Tailscale-reachable endpoint (either a node hostname or the VIP if it is also advertised on the tailnet). Check `kubectl config view --minify` to confirm the server URL before troubleshooting connectivity.

## What Not To Commit

Per the top-level `CLAUDE.md` rule: **never write the real tailnet name into any file under git.** Use the placeholder `<tailnet-name>.ts.net` in documentation, examples, and commit messages. MagicDNS short hostnames (`k8s-1` etc.) are fine — they are not tailnet-identifying on their own. Tailscale `100.x.x.x` IPs should also be avoided in committed files.

## Troubleshooting

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `failed to determine endpoints` | no `-e` flag and default config has no context | pass `--talosconfig ./talos/clusterconfig/talosconfig -e <host>` |
| `talos config file is empty` | `~/.talos/config` has never been populated | same — point at repo's talosconfig explicitly |
| `i/o timeout` on a 192.168.x.x IP | off-LAN; LAN addresses unreachable | switch to Tailscale MagicDNS hostname |
| `server version X is older than client version Y` warning | client/server skew, harmless until a major release | plan a Talos upgrade via `task talos:upgrade-node` |
| Tailscale host not resolving | device signed out / key expired | re-auth the node's Tailscale extension (see `talos/patches/<hostname>/`) |
